definition: Phillips Curve convexity near the zero output gap (CBO estimate -0.3% to +0.3%) makes the current economy maximally sensitive to small shocks, producing outsized correlation swings of 0.15-0.25 in rolling 3-month windows.
evidence: Grounded in Ball-Mankiw-Romer (1988) and Hazell et al. (2022)
Firms adjust prices more aggressively to cost shocks when operating near capacity
Provides structural explanation for observed correlation instability
Ball-Mankiw-Romer 1988 and Hazell et al. 2022 theoretical grounding for convexity
Covenant-lite (>90% of leveraged loans) with 15-40% EBITDA addbacks delays default-transmission channel by 12-24 months
Complementary explanations from macro theory and credit market structure
Halving the gap from -2pp to -1pp adds more inflation than from -1pp to -0.5pp
1999-2000, 2006-2007, 2018-2019 all show accelerating pressure at low unemployment
Getting from 2.6% to 2.0% inflation may require 0.5-1.0pp more unemployment than linear models predict
open_questions: Limited empirical validation at this specific output gap level
Whether the convexity relationship is stable across different inflation regimes
Is the 'immaculate disinflation' evidence of a flat curve with supply normalization rather than a convex curve?
Is the Phillips Curve slope itself endogenous to the anchoring state (circularity problem)?
Can the 0.5-1.0pp additional unemployment estimate for last-mile disinflation be more sharply calibrated?
Does immigration reversal shift the curve or change its shape?
Is convexity stable under AI-driven productivity changes?
